narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and personal circumstances to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 34 (under the usual 36.7), and low decile rankings in Year 1 and Year 3 at school—and predicted a 74.3% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tier compared to peers and strongly signaled lower academic performance relative to what the model associates with bar success. Your Year 1 decile ranking of 1 reinforced this concern, further elevating the risk in the model's eyes by showing consistently low standing early on. On a more positive note, your LSAT score worked in your favor, pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing since it was reasonably competitive, though not enough to offset the other red flags. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your grades put you well below the pack, while full-time enrollment status aligned with the vast majority (over 90%) but didn't sway the outcome much here. This combination made your profile stand out as higher-risk than average applicants who get approved. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergraduate record through a post-bac program or additional coursework, and targeting schools with more holistic reviews that might weigh your full-time commitment and background more favorably. Building a stronger narrative around your resilience as a middle-income student could also help in future appeals or reapplications. With targeted improvements, you can shift your predicted odds and open new doors.
